proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. Furthermore, the technical implementation plan is vague, suggesting a dependency on Google to develop a custom app and the government to unilaterally modify bus routes and enforce home-office policies, which are complex systemic interventions unlikely to occur based on a single awareness event. The causal chain from 'one-day walking' to 'permanent habit change' lacks empirical grounding in behavioral science literature.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal does not introduce a novel mechanism for behavior change or a new technological approach; it repurposes existing concepts (tracking steps, calculating footprints) without addressing why previous similar initiatives have struggled to achieve long-term retention or significant emission reductions beyond the event day.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically consistent with the stated assumptions but represents a negligible fraction of the city's total emissions. The long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound; the proposal extrapolates a one-day behavioral shift into permanent structural changes (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 2% electric bus fleet adoption, 1% solar coverage) without providing a causal mechanism or evidence that a single awareness event drives such policy or infrastructure shifts. The claim that this event will lead to 39 million tCO2e/year globally is a speculative extrapolation unsupported by the pilot data.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing. However, the presentation of data is often misleading due to the conflation of short-term event metrics with long-term systemic outcomes. The reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute critical components (app development, route changes) is presented as a certainty rather than a risk, and the citation of sources is inconsistent (e.g., referencing 'Government web page [14]' without specific context).

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term awareness campaign with long-term systemic transformation. While the immediate emission savings from a single day of reduced car use are calculable, the causal mechanisms linking this event to the proposed long-term policy changes (solar law approval, bus electrification) are tenuous and lack scientific plausibility. The proposal suffers from significant uncertainty regarding participant uptake and the feasibility of the required institutional coordination.
key_concerns: The assumption that a one-day event will causally drive complex, long-term policy changes (e.g., solar law approval, bus fleet electrification) is not supported by empirical evidence.
The 5% participation rate is an unverified assumption that likely overestimates voluntary engagement for a single-day event without incentives or structural barriers removed.

The proposal assumes the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) and government route modifications as guaranteed outcomes, ignoring significant technical and bureaucratic hurdles.
The long-term impact calculations appear to double-count or conflate independent policy trends with the specific impact of this event, inflating the projected efficacy.
There is no evidence provided regarding the 'habit formation' duration; behavioral science suggests single-day events rarely result in permanent lifestyle changes without sustained reinforcement.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers. It assumes the government can unilaterally 'notify' companies to allow home office and 'arrange' transportation route modifications for a single day without a formal legislative or regulatory framework. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom tracking app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a one-off event without a robust mobilization strategy significantly lowers regulatory and operational feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a custom data-tracking app and the attempt to link this single event to long-term policy outcomes (like solar law approval), but the governance mechanism itself is not innovative.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are plausible for a single day, the long-term impact projections are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal assumes that a one-day awareness event will automatically trigger a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of distributed solar laws, and a 2% shift to electric buses. These are complex policy outcomes requiring years of legislative work, not a direct result of a single awareness day.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant logical gaps in its impact modeling and governance assumptions. The text references images (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') that are not visible, and the calculation logic for long-term impacts conflates correlation with causation, weakening the persuasive power of the argument for policymakers.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates the ability of a single event to drive structural policy change. While the intent to reduce emissions and promote health is sound, the institutional levers required to achieve the stated long-term goals (legislative approval, corporate policy shifts) are not addressed with realistic regulatory strategies. The proposal treats complex systemic changes as automatic byproducts of a one-day event, which is a significant governance gap.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes government authority to mandate corporate home-office policies and transport route changes without detailing the legal or political process required to secure such cooperation.
There is no clear accountability mechanism or regulatory framework to ensure the 'Big Data' collected leads to actual infrastructure improvements or policy changes.

The long-term impact calculations (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus implementation) are presented as direct outcomes of the event, ignoring the multi-year legislative and budgetary cycles required for such changes.

Reliance on a major tech company (Google) to develop a custom app and manage data raises questions about data privacy, liability, and the realistic feasibility of such a partnership without a formal public-private agreement.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with high coordination friction. It assumes government authorization to modify bus routes and mandate home-office days, which is operationally unrealistic for a one-off event. Furthermore, the technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while expecting Google to develop it for free, presents a significant resource and dependency risk. The reliance on 5% of the population participating voluntarily on a specific day without a robust incentive structure beyond 'altruism' and vague prizes makes execution highly fragile.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a layer of data tracking via a smartphone app, but the underlying behavioral intervention is not novel. The approach of using existing 'ecobici' infrastructure and standard carbon footprint algorithms does not introduce a new mechanism for change, but rather repackages existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and temporary, as it relies on a single day of behavior change. The long-term impact projections are mathematically flawed and overly optimistic; the proposal extrapolates a one-day event's data to predict a 10% increase in recycling, a 5% solar adoption rate, and a 2% electric bus fleet conversion over 5 years. These systemic infrastructure and policy changes cannot be causally attributed to a single awareness day without a detailed, funded implementation roadmap for those specific policy shifts.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the impact analysis is confusing and contains significant logical leaps. The distinction between the event's direct impact and the long-term policy outcomes is blurred, leading to inflated numbers. The inclusion of business model canvas images and references to external articles shows effort, but the narrative fails to clearly separate the 'event' from the 'policy advocacy' required to achieve the stated long-term goals.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that overestimates its ability to drive systemic infrastructure change. While the intent to promote walking and cycling is sound, the operational plan lacks the necessary leverage to secure government route changes or corporate home-office mandates. The financial and technical dependencies (Google developing an app, government funding security/ambulance) are not grounded in a realistic resource model. The long-term impact claims are speculative and disconnected from the actual scope of a one-day event.
key_concerns: Operational feasibility of securing government authorization to modify public transport routes for a single day.
Lack of a concrete funding model for app development, security, and medical support; reliance on 'Google' to build the app is a critical risk.
Logical gap between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus fleet conversion).
Incentive structure is weak; 'altruism' and vague prizes are unlikely to drive 5% population participation in a dense city like Buenos Aires.
Data privacy and liability concerns regarding tracking user locations and health data without a clear governance framework.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'government authorization' to modify bus routes and a 'Google-developed' app that does not exist, creating a high barrier to entry. Crucially, the instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is practically unfeasible for low-income residents in Buenos Aires who rely on public transit for survival; asking them to walk 10-15 blocks (often 1-2 km) in a dense, hot city without infrastructure support is unrealistic for the working class.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days and gamified carbon tracking is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work). The novelty here is limited to the specific context of Buenos Aires and the attempt to aggregate 'big data' for urban planning, but the core mechanism is a standard awareness campaign rather than a structural innovation.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e) are calculated, the long-term impact assumptions are dangerously optimistic and disconnected from reality. The proposal assumes a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% shift to electric buses will result directly from a single walking day, which ignores the systemic barriers (cost, infrastructure, policy) that actually drive those changes. The impact is likely to be a one-day novelty with negligible habit formation for the majority.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant logical gaps and tone-deafness regarding the target audience. The text contradicts itself by asking people to 'skip the bus' while simultaneously claiming the event will 'incentivize the use of public transport.' The reliance on hypothetical government actions and unproven app development weakens the credibility of the presentation.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down initiative that misunderstands the daily reality of Buenos Aires residents. It asks the most vulnerable populations to make impossible choices (walking long distances instead of taking a bus) under the guise of altruism, while promising systemic changes (electric buses, recycling laws) that a single event cannot deliver. The 'skip the bus' directive is particularly alienating to those who cannot afford to walk or cycle due to distance, weather, or safety concerns.
key_concerns: The directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is exclusionary and dangerous for low-income workers who cannot walk 10-15 blocks to work.
The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (modifying routes, home office mandates) that is not guaranteed and requires complex negotiation not addressed in the plan.
There is no mention of safety infrastructure (sidewalks, lighting, bike lanes) which is critical for walking/cycling in a dense city like Buenos Aires.
The 'big data' collection via a smartphone app excludes the elderly, the poor, and those without smartphones, skewing the data and the perceived impact.
The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are not causally linked to a one-day walking event and appear to be wishful thinking.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly coerce companies to allow home office and modify public transport routes for a single-day event. The text states the government must 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes,' which is logistically and politically improbable for a one-off awareness day without a long-term policy framework. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization creates a fragile dependency that undermines operational feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism and value proposition are derivative of existing campaigns, offering little new methodological or conceptual novelty.

impact.rationale: The proposal identifies a clear potential for short-term behavioral shifts and health benefits (reduced sedentary lifestyle). However, the long-term impact projections are normatively flawed and mathematically suspect. The proposal claims a 10% increase in recycling and a 5% city-wide solar adoption as direct results of a single walking day, which represents a severe overestimation of causal linkage. The 'impact' is likely to be symbolic and temporary rather than structural, failing to address the systemic barriers to sustainable transport for low-income populations who cannot afford to walk long distances or work from home.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is enthusiastic but structurally disjointed, mixing narrative with raw calculations and broken image references (e.g., 'main-objections.png'). While the intent to use data and media is clear, the argumentation lacks rigor in connecting the event to the massive long-term policy changes it predicts. The tone is optimistic but lacks the critical depth required to address the complex trade-offs of urban mobility and social equity.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day event to trigger systemic policy changes, but it obscures significant value conflicts regarding social equity and the feasibility of government mandates. While the health and awareness benefits are real, the proposal risks excluding low-income workers who rely on public transit for long commutes and cannot work from home. The projected long-term impacts (e.g., solar law approval, recycling spikes) are presented as inevitable outcomes of a single event, masking the deep structural and political trade-offs required to achieve them.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes all participants can walk to work or work from home, ignoring low-income populations who live in peripheral areas with poor connectivity and must use public transit for long distances.

Normative Overreach: The causal link between a one-day walking event and complex policy outcomes (e.g., passing a solar law, increasing recycling by 10%) is tenuous and lacks a realistic implementation pathway.

Data Privacy & Equity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track movement and award prizes may exclude those without smartphones or data plans, creating a 'digital divide' in who can participate and benefit.

Government Coercion: The feasibility of the government forcing companies to allow home office or altering bus routes for a single day is highly questionable and may face significant political resistance.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus adoption, 10% recycling increase) and massive behavioral changes (weekly home office). The assumption that a one-off 'awareness day' can force government infrastructure changes or alter long-term commuting habits without a sustained, multi-year intervention strategy is structurally weak. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization for route modifications introduces significant execution risks that are not mitigated in the plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (as acknowledged by the proposal's own review of US/Canada programs). While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism—using a single day of voluntary behavior change to drive long-term systemic impact—is not novel. It replicates existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new systemic lever or a unique feedback loop for resilience.

impact.rationale: The proposal conflates short-term, one-day emission reductions (66 tCO2e) with long-term, structural decarbonization (39 million tCO2e/year). From a long-term impact lens, the projected 10% increase in recycling and 2% electric bus adoption are presented as direct, automatic consequences of a single event, which ignores the complex, slow-moving nature of infrastructure development and cultural shifts. The proposal risks 'shifting problems' by creating a temporary drop in emissions while failing to address the systemic rigidity of urban planning that necessitates car use for the majority of the population. True resilience requires permanent infrastructure, not temporary behavioral spikes.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to quantify impacts, which is commendable. However, the presentation of long-term impact calculations is logically flawed, extrapolating a one-day event's data into multi-year systemic outcomes without a clear mechanism for how the transition occurs. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the somewhat disjointed flow between the event description and the massive long-term forecasts weaken the overall coherence of the argument.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a single-day awareness event to trigger profound, long-term systemic changes in urban mobility and energy consumption. While the immediate ecological benefit of reduced car use on the event day is real, the proposal's core weakness lies in its assumption that a temporary behavioral shift can automatically catalyze permanent infrastructure upgrades and cultural transformations. From a long-term resilience perspective, the plan lacks a robust mechanism to sustain momentum beyond the event, risking a 'one-off' impact that fails to build the structural capacity needed for genuine climate adaptation.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (e.g., electric bus adoption, recycling laws) is speculative and lacks a defined implementation pathway.

The proposal assumes that 'awareness' alone is sufficient to overcome structural barriers to walking/cycling (e.g., safety, distance, lack of infrastructure) without detailing how these barriers will be permanently removed.
There is a risk of 'greenwashing' if the event creates a temporary drop in emissions while the underlying car-dependent urban design remains unchanged.
The reliance on a single-day metric to justify multi-year projections (39 million tCO2e) is methodologically unsound for long-term impact assessment.
The proposal does not address the potential for 'rebound effects' where participants return to high-carbon behaviors immediately after the event.

A1.text: The peer reviews (A2-A6) significantly reinforce my initial assessment of low feasibility and impact, while adding critical dimensions I had only briefly noted. Specifically, A4's identification of the 'skip the bus' directive as exclusionary for low-income populations and A5's analysis of the 'digital divide' regarding the smartphone app requirement are vital empirical corrections. These points confirm that the proposal's causal mechanism for participation is not just optimistic, but structurally flawed for the target demographic in Buenos Aires. Furthermore, A6's framing of the long-term projections as a potential 'greenwashing' risk due to the lack of sustained infrastructure investment aligns with my concern about the tenuous link between a one-day event and systemic policy shifts (e.g., solar law approval). The consensus on the lack of a realistic governance pathway for the proposed government mandates (A2, A3, A5) solidifies the view that the proposal rests on speculative rather than empirical foundations. My confidence in the low scores for feasibility and impact has increased to >0.95. No score revision is necessary as the peer inputs validate, rather than contradict, my initial quantitative and qualitative judgments.

A2.text: The peer review strongly reinforces my initial governance assessment. A4's focus on social equity and the 'skip the bus' directive adds a critical dimension: the proposal not only lacks regulatory feasibility but also risks political backlash by ignoring the mobility constraints of low-income populations. A5 and A6 further clarify that the 'causal leap' from a one-day event to complex legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification) is a fundamental governance gap, not just a calculation error. The consensus on the unrealistic dependency on Google and the lack of a formal public-private framework solidifies the low feasibility score. I maintain my original scores, as the pool confirms that the institutional levers required are neither present nor realistically mobilizable under the current proposal structure.

A3.text: The peer reviews, particularly A4 and A5, critically reinforce my initial feasibility concerns by highlighting the 'exclusionary' nature of the 'skip the bus' directive and the 'digital divide' in app-based tracking. A4's point that low-income workers cannot simply walk 10-15 blocks in a dense, hot city without infrastructure support is a fatal operational flaw I underweighted. Furthermore, A6's warning about 'greenwashing' and the lack of a sustained mechanism beyond the event day validates my skepticism regarding the long-term impact scores. The consensus that the proposal conflates a one-day awareness campaign with multi-year policy outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification) is robust. While I maintain my low scores for feasibility and novelty, the clarity provided by the peers on the social equity risks and the specific logical fallacies in the impact modeling confirms that the current proposal is not operationally viable as written. No score revision is needed as the initial assessment already reflected these systemic gaps, but the peer input solidifies the rationale for the low impact score.

A4.text: Reading the pool, I find strong alignment with A1, A2, and A3 regarding the systemic overreach of linking a one-day event to complex policy outcomes like solar law approval or bus electrification. However, A5 and A6 add a crucial layer regarding the 'digital divide' and the specific exclusion of low-income workers who cannot afford to walk long distances or work from home. This reinforces my concern about the 'skip the bus' directive: for many in Buenos Aires, the bus is not a luxury to be skipped but a necessity. The proposal's reliance on a smartphone app for tracking and prizes further alienates the elderly and the poor, skewing the data and the perceived 'success' of the event. While the health benefits are real, the current framing risks being a top-down exercise that ignores the daily survival realities of the most vulnerable. I maintain my scores as they already reflect these ground-level frictions, but I emphasize that the 'impact' score of 2 is specifically because the proposal fails to account for who is *not* able to participate, rendering the 'awareness' message inaccessible to those who need it most.

A5.text: The pool powerfully reinforces my initial concerns regarding exclusion and normative trade-offs. A4's specific critique of the 'skip the bus' directive is crucial: it exposes how the proposal's 'altruistic' framing masks a class-based exclusion, effectively penalizing low-income workers who cannot walk long distances or work from home. A1, A2, and A6's rigorous dismantling of the causal chain between a one-day event and complex policy shifts (solar laws, bus electrification) confirms that the proposal's 'impact' is largely speculative and risks 'greenwashing' by conflating temporary behavioral spikes with structural change. While I initially scored Impact at 3 based on the potential for health awareness, the consensus that the long-term projections are mathematically unsound and the short-term benefits are inaccessible to the most vulnerable necessitates a downward revision. The proposal hides a severe value conflict: it prioritizes symbolic visibility over equitable access to mobility and ignores the structural barriers that make walking impossible for many.

A6.text: The perspectives from A4 (social equity) and A5 (normative overreach) critically deepen my initial assessment. A4's point that 'skipping the bus' excludes low-income workers who cannot walk long distances highlights a systemic resilience failure: the proposal risks reinforcing existing inequalities rather than building inclusive adaptation. A5's identification of the 'digital divide' further confirms that the data-driven approach may skew participation toward the privileged, undermining the 'long-term societal effects' I was evaluating. While I already flagged the weak causal link between a one-day event and structural policy changes, these peers clarify that the proposal's assumptions about 'altruism' ignore the material constraints of the most vulnerable populations. This reinforces my view that the project, as currently framed, shifts the burden of sustainability onto individuals without addressing the structural rigidity of the city. I maintain my scores, as the fundamental disconnect between the short-term event and the claimed long-term systemic resilience remains unaddressed, but the equity lens provided by A4 and A5 is essential for a complete synthesis.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event without significant logistical friction. Furthermore, the technical implementation plan is vague, suggesting a dependency on Google to develop a custom app and the government to unilaterally modify bus routes and enforce home-office policies, which are complex systemic interventions unlikely to occur based on a single awareness event. The causal chain from 'one-day walking' to 'permanent habit change' lacks empirical grounding in behavioral science literature.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Month). The proposal does not introduce a novel mechanism for behavior change or a new technological approach; it repurposes existing concepts (tracking steps, calculating footprints) without addressing why previous similar initiatives have struggled to achieve long-term retention or significant emission reductions beyond the event day.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically consistent with the stated assumptions but represents a negligible fraction of the city's total emissions. The long-term impact projections are scientifically unsound; the proposal extrapolates a one-day behavioral shift into permanent structural changes (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 2% electric bus fleet adoption, 1% solar coverage) without providing a causal mechanism or evidence that a single awareness event drives such policy or infrastructure shifts. The claim that this event will lead to 39 million tCO2e/year globally is a speculative extrapolation unsupported by the pilot data.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing. However, the presentation of data is often misleading due to the conflation of short-term event metrics with long-term systemic outcomes. The reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute critical components (app development, route changes) is presented as a certainty rather than a risk, and the citation of sources is inconsistent (e.g., referencing 'Government web page [14]' without specific context).

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a short-term awareness campaign with long-term systemic transformation. While the immediate emission savings from a single day of reduced car use are calculable, the causal mechanisms linking this event to the proposed long-term policy changes (solar law approval, bus electrification) are tenuous and lack scientific plausibility. The proposal suffers from significant uncertainty regarding participant uptake and the feasibility of the required institutional coordination.
key_concerns: The assumption that a one-day event will causally drive complex, long-term policy changes (e.g., solar law approval, bus fleet electrification) is not supported by empirical evidence.
The 5% participation rate is an unverified assumption that likely overestimates voluntary engagement for a single-day event without incentives or structural barriers removed.

The proposal assumes the development of a custom tracking app by a major tech giant (Google) and government route modifications as guaranteed outcomes, ignoring significant technical and bureaucratic hurdles.
The long-term impact calculations appear to double-count or conflate independent policy trends with the specific impact of this event, inflating the projected efficacy.
There is no evidence provided regarding the 'habit formation' duration; behavioral science suggests single-day events rarely result in permanent lifestyle changes without sustained reinforcement.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers. It assumes the government can unilaterally 'notify' companies to allow home office and 'arrange' transportation route modifications for a single day without a formal legislative or regulatory framework. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom tracking app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily participate in a one-off event without a robust mobilization strategy significantly lowers regulatory and operational feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific integration of a custom data-tracking app and the attempt to link this single event to long-term policy outcomes (like solar law approval), but the governance mechanism itself is not innovative.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are plausible for a single day, the long-term impact projections are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal assumes that a one-day awareness event will automatically trigger a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of distributed solar laws, and a 2% shift to electric buses. These are complex policy outcomes requiring years of legislative work, not a direct result of a single awareness day.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant logical gaps in its impact modeling and governance assumptions. The text references images (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') that are not visible, and the calculation logic for long-term impacts conflates correlation with causation, weakening the persuasive power of the argument for policymakers.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates the ability of a single event to drive structural policy change. While the intent to reduce emissions and promote health is sound, the institutional levers required to achieve the stated long-term goals (legislative approval, corporate policy shifts) are not addressed with realistic regulatory strategies. The proposal treats complex systemic changes as automatic byproducts of a one-day event, which is a significant governance gap.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes government authority to mandate corporate home-office policies and transport route changes without detailing the legal or political process required to secure such cooperation.
There is no clear accountability mechanism or regulatory framework to ensure the 'Big Data' collected leads to actual infrastructure improvements or policy changes.

The long-term impact calculations (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus implementation) are presented as direct outcomes of the event, ignoring the multi-year legislative and budgetary cycles required for such changes.

Reliance on a major tech company (Google) to develop a custom app and manage data raises questions about data privacy, liability, and the realistic feasibility of such a partnership without a formal public-private agreement.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with high coordination friction. It assumes government authorization to modify bus routes and mandate home-office days, which is operationally unrealistic for a one-off event. Furthermore, the technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while expecting Google to develop it for free, presents a significant resource and dependency risk. The reliance on 5% of the population participating voluntarily on a specific day without a robust incentive structure beyond 'altruism' and vague prizes makes execution highly fragile.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal adds a layer of data tracking via a smartphone app, but the underlying behavioral intervention is not novel. The approach of using existing 'ecobici' infrastructure and standard carbon footprint algorithms does not introduce a new mechanism for change, but rather repackages existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and temporary, as it relies on a single day of behavior change. The long-term impact projections are mathematically flawed and overly optimistic; the proposal extrapolates a one-day event's data to predict a 10% increase in recycling, a 5% solar adoption rate, and a 2% electric bus fleet conversion over 5 years. These systemic infrastructure and policy changes cannot be causally attributed to a single awareness day without a detailed, funded implementation roadmap for those specific policy shifts.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the presentation of the impact analysis is confusing and contains significant logical leaps. The distinction between the event's direct impact and the long-term policy outcomes is blurred, leading to inflated numbers. The inclusion of business model canvas images and references to external articles shows effort, but the narrative fails to clearly separate the 'event' from the 'policy advocacy' required to achieve the stated long-term goals.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that overestimates its ability to drive systemic infrastructure change. While the intent to promote walking and cycling is sound, the operational plan lacks the necessary leverage to secure government route changes or corporate home-office mandates. The financial and technical dependencies (Google developing an app, government funding security/ambulance) are not grounded in a realistic resource model. The long-term impact claims are speculative and disconnected from the actual scope of a one-day event.
key_concerns: Operational feasibility of securing government authorization to modify public transport routes for a single day.
Lack of a concrete funding model for app development, security, and medical support; reliance on 'Google' to build the app is a critical risk.
Logical gap between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus fleet conversion).
Incentive structure is weak; 'altruism' and vague prizes are unlikely to drive 5% population participation in a dense city like Buenos Aires.
Data privacy and liability concerns regarding tracking user locations and health data without a clear governance framework.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'government authorization' to modify bus routes and a 'Google-developed' app that does not exist, creating a high barrier to entry. Crucially, the instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is practically unfeasible for low-income residents in Buenos Aires who rely on public transit for survival; asking them to walk 10-15 blocks (often 1-2 km) in a dense, hot city without infrastructure support is unrealistic for the working class.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' days and gamified carbon tracking is well-established globally (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work). The novelty here is limited to the specific context of Buenos Aires and the attempt to aggregate 'big data' for urban planning, but the core mechanism is a standard awareness campaign rather than a structural innovation.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e) are calculated, the long-term impact assumptions are dangerously optimistic and disconnected from reality. The proposal assumes a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% shift to electric buses will result directly from a single walking day, which ignores the systemic barriers (cost, infrastructure, policy) that actually drive those changes. The impact is likely to be a one-day novelty with negligible habit formation for the majority.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant logical gaps and tone-deafness regarding the target audience. The text contradicts itself by asking people to 'skip the bus' while simultaneously claiming the event will 'incentivize the use of public transport.' The reliance on hypothetical government actions and unproven app development weakens the credibility of the presentation.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down initiative that misunderstands the daily reality of Buenos Aires residents. It asks the most vulnerable populations to make impossible choices (walking long distances instead of taking a bus) under the guise of altruism, while promising systemic changes (electric buses, recycling laws) that a single event cannot deliver. The 'skip the bus' directive is particularly alienating to those who cannot afford to walk or cycle due to distance, weather, or safety concerns.
key_concerns: The directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is exclusionary and dangerous for low-income workers who cannot walk 10-15 blocks to work.
The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (modifying routes, home office mandates) that is not guaranteed and requires complex negotiation not addressed in the plan.
There is no mention of safety infrastructure (sidewalks, lighting, bike lanes) which is critical for walking/cycling in a dense city like Buenos Aires.
The 'big data' collection via a smartphone app excludes the elderly, the poor, and those without smartphones, skewing the data and the perceived impact.
The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are not causally linked to a one-day walking event and appear to be wishful thinking.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly coerce companies to allow home office and modify public transport routes for a single-day event. The text states the government must 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes,' which is logistically and politically improbable for a one-off awareness day without a long-term policy framework. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization creates a fragile dependency that undermines operational feasibility.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism and value proposition are derivative of existing campaigns, offering little new methodological or conceptual novelty.

impact.rationale: The proposal identifies a clear potential for short-term behavioral shifts and health benefits (reduced sedentary lifestyle). However, the long-term impact projections are normatively flawed and mathematically suspect. The proposal claims a 10% increase in recycling and a 5% city-wide solar adoption as direct results of a single walking day, which represents a severe overestimation of causal linkage. The 'impact' is likely to be symbolic and temporary rather than structural, failing to address the systemic barriers to sustainable transport for low-income populations who cannot afford to walk long distances or work from home.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is enthusiastic but structurally disjointed, mixing narrative with raw calculations and broken image references (e.g., 'main-objections.png'). While the intent to use data and media is clear, the argumentation lacks rigor in connecting the event to the massive long-term policy changes it predicts. The tone is optimistic but lacks the critical depth required to address the complex trade-offs of urban mobility and social equity.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a one-day event to trigger systemic policy changes, but it obscures significant value conflicts regarding social equity and the feasibility of government mandates. While the health and awareness benefits are real, the proposal risks excluding low-income workers who rely on public transit for long commutes and cannot work from home. The projected long-term impacts (e.g., solar law approval, recycling spikes) are presented as inevitable outcomes of a single event, masking the deep structural and political trade-offs required to achieve them.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes all participants can walk to work or work from home, ignoring low-income populations who live in peripheral areas with poor connectivity and must use public transit for long distances.

Normative Overreach: The causal link between a one-day walking event and complex policy outcomes (e.g., passing a solar law, increasing recycling by 10%) is tenuous and lacks a realistic implementation pathway.

Data Privacy & Equity: The reliance on a smartphone app to track movement and award prizes may exclude those without smartphones or data plans, creating a 'digital divide' in who can participate and benefit.

Government Coercion: The feasibility of the government forcing companies to allow home office or altering bus routes for a single day is highly questionable and may face significant political resistance.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus adoption, 10% recycling increase) and massive behavioral changes (weekly home office). The assumption that a one-off 'awareness day' can force government infrastructure changes or alter long-term commuting habits without a sustained, multi-year intervention strategy is structurally weak. Furthermore, the reliance on a 'Google-developed' app and immediate government authorization for route modifications introduces significant execution risks that are not mitigated in the plan.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (as acknowledged by the proposal's own review of US/Canada programs). While the integration of a tracking app and the specific focus on Buenos Aires adds local context, the fundamental mechanism—using a single day of voluntary behavior change to drive long-term systemic impact—is not novel. It replicates existing awareness campaigns without introducing a new systemic lever or a unique feedback loop for resilience.

impact.rationale: The proposal conflates short-term, one-day emission reductions (66 tCO2e) with long-term, structural decarbonization (39 million tCO2e/year). From a long-term impact lens, the projected 10% increase in recycling and 2% electric bus adoption are presented as direct, automatic consequences of a single event, which ignores the complex, slow-moving nature of infrastructure development and cultural shifts. The proposal risks 'shifting problems' by creating a temporary drop in emissions while failing to address the systemic rigidity of urban planning that necessitates car use for the majority of the population. True resilience requires permanent infrastructure, not temporary behavioral spikes.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to quantify impacts, which is commendable. However, the presentation of long-term impact calculations is logically flawed, extrapolating a one-day event's data into multi-year systemic outcomes without a clear mechanism for how the transition occurs. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the somewhat disjointed flow between the event description and the massive long-term forecasts weaken the overall coherence of the argument.

A6.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage a single-day awareness event to trigger profound, long-term systemic changes in urban mobility and energy consumption. While the immediate ecological benefit of reduced car use on the event day is real, the proposal's core weakness lies in its assumption that a temporary behavioral shift can automatically catalyze permanent infrastructure upgrades and cultural transformations. From a long-term resilience perspective, the plan lacks a robust mechanism to sustain momentum beyond the event, risking a 'one-off' impact that fails to build the structural capacity needed for genuine climate adaptation.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (e.g., electric bus adoption, recycling laws) is speculative and lacks a defined implementation pathway.

The proposal assumes that 'awareness' alone is sufficient to overcome structural barriers to walking/cycling (e.g., safety, distance, lack of infrastructure) without detailing how these barriers will be permanently removed.
There is a risk of 'greenwashing' if the event creates a temporary drop in emissions while the underlying car-dependent urban design remains unchanged.
The reliance on a single-day metric to justify multi-year projections (39 million tCO2e) is methodologically unsound for long-term impact assessment.
The proposal does not address the potential for 'rebound effects' where participants return to high-carbon behaviors immediately after the event.

clusters.label: The Causal Gap: One-Day Events vs. Structural Policy Change
clusters.summary: All experts converge on the view that the proposal's core logical flaw is the assumption that a single-day awareness event can causally trigger complex, long-term systemic changes (e.g., solar law approval, 10% recycling increase, electric bus fleet conversion). The consensus is that these projections are speculative, lack empirical grounding, and conflate correlation with causation, rendering the long-term impact claims scientifically unsound.

clusters.label: Operational Fragility and Unrealistic Institutional Dependencies
clusters.summary: Experts identify a critical feasibility gap regarding the proposal's reliance on unverified institutional levers. The plan assumes the government will unilaterally modify bus routes, mandate home-office policies, and that a major tech giant (Google) will develop a custom app for free. These dependencies are viewed as operationally unrealistic for a one-off event without a formal regulatory framework or public-private agreement, creating a high risk of execution failure.

clusters.label: Social Equity and the 'Skip the Bus' Paradox
clusters.summary: A critical dimension of critique focuses on the proposal's exclusionary nature. Experts highlight that the directive to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' disproportionately affects low-income populations who rely on public transit for survival and cannot walk long distances. Furthermore, the reliance on a smartphone app for tracking and prizes creates a 'digital divide,' excluding the elderly and the poor, thereby skewing data and undermining the proposal's claim of broad societal impact.

clusters.label: Derivative Concept and Lack of Novelty
clusters.summary: The pool agrees that the core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice. While the proposal attempts to add novelty through data tracking and local context (Buenos Aires), the underlying behavioral intervention and value proposition are viewed as repackaged existing campaigns rather than innovative mechanisms for change.

open_questions: What specific, evidence-based mechanisms could bridge the gap between a single-day behavioral event and the long-term policy outcomes (e.g., solar law, bus electrification) claimed in the proposal?

How can the proposal be redesigned to ensure inclusivity for low-income populations and those without smartphones, avoiding the 'digital divide' and the exclusionary effects of the 'skip the bus' directive?

What is a realistic, phased implementation roadmap for the institutional dependencies (government route changes, corporate home-office mandates) that does not rely on unverified assumptions of immediate cooperation?
If the long-term impact projections are removed or significantly revised, what is the standalone value proposition of the event in terms of immediate emission reductions and health benefits?

How can the proposal address the 'greenwashing' risk identified by experts, ensuring that the event contributes to genuine structural resilience rather than just a temporary symbolic drop in emissions?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on highly unrealistic institutional dependencies, assuming the government will unilaterally modify bus routes and mandate corporate home-office policies for a single day, and that Google will develop a custom tracking app without a formal agreement. The operational plan lacks a realistic funding model and ignores the logistical friction of coordinating 5% of a dense city's population for a one-off event.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice. While the proposal attempts to integrate data tracking and local context (Buenos Aires), the underlying behavioral intervention and value proposition are derivative of existing campaigns, offering no new mechanism for systemic change.

impact.rationale: Short-term emission savings are calculable but negligible relative to total city emissions. The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase, solar law approval, electric bus adoption) are scientifically unsound, as they falsely attribute complex, multi-year policy and infrastructure shifts to a single-day awareness event without a causal mechanism.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use quantitative data. However, the presentation is weakened by logical gaps in impact modeling, the conflation of short-term metrics with long-term systemic outcomes, and the inclusion of broken image references. The tone is optimistic but lacks the critical depth required to address social equity and implementation risks.

final.synthesis: The final judgment integrates a strong consensus across all six experts (A1-A6) that the proposal suffers from a fundamental 'causal gap': it assumes a one-day event can automatically trigger complex, long-term structural changes (policy, infrastructure, culture) without a defined implementation pathway. Experts A1, A2, A3, A5, and A6 uniformly flagged the long-term impact projections as speculative and methodologically flawed. Regarding feasibility, the consensus (A1-A6) highlights the fragility of relying on unverified government mandates and a hypothetical Google app. A critical divergence in perspective was noted regarding social equity (A4, A5), where experts emphasized that the directive to 'skip the bus' is exclusionary to low-income populations, a nuance that reinforces the feasibility score by highlighting a barrier to the proposed 5% participation rate. The novelty score was uniformly low (2) as the concept is a repackaging of existing global initiatives. The presentation score (3) reflects the proposal's structural clarity despite its logical flaws. The final scores reflect a proposal with good intent but poor execution logic and unrealistic assumptions.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty remains the specific mechanism by which the organizers intend to secure government cooperation for route modifications and corporate home-office mandates, as the proposal treats these as guaranteed rather than negotiated outcomes. Additionally, while experts agree the long-term impact claims are inflated, there is no consensus on the exact magnitude of the short-term health and awareness benefits, which are difficult to quantify without pilot data. The potential for the 'big data' component to yield genuine urban planning insights remains an open question, contingent on the feasibility of the app development and data privacy frameworks.
